turns.content:
Dựa trên phân tích toàn diện codebase maps (ARCHITECTURE.md, STRUCTURE.md, CONCERNS.md) và trạng thái hiện tại của các CQRS boundaries, source-plane boundaries, và boundary violations đã biết, đây là kết quả nghiên cứu gray area:

---

## Boundary Sync + Manifest Update

| Option | Pros | Cons | Complexity | Recommendation |
|--------|------|------|------------|----------------|
| **Verify-then-cleanup** — Boundary audit first (grep boundary drifts against `last_mapped_commit`), update manifests with verified changes, then cleanup knowing precise constraints | Catches undocumented boundary shifts early; prevents cleanups from violating constraints that manifests don't reflect; produces auditable gap report; maps stay grounded in actual drift | Extra round-trip before cleanup starts; audit may surface false positives from in-progress work; depends on `last_mapped_commit` accuracy | ~8 files (ARCHITECTURE, STRUCTURE, AGENTS.md × 4, CONCERNS, CONVENTIONS, TESTING) — Risk: audit inflation, false positives in CQRS detection | Rec if boundary violations are known-unknown (e.g., CommandDelegationHandler CQRS split already flagged in CONCERNS.md — this confirms actual scope before writing) |
| **Batch cleanup+sync** — Combined pass: as each dead-code removal or boundary fix completes, update relevant manifest sections immediately | Single wave, minimal round-trips; manifests stay approximately correct during work; natural scope pairing (fix X → update X's doc) | Verification bias: cleaning mindset trumps boundary-discovery mindset — may miss shifts outside the cleanup scope; commit granularity harder to enforce; diff noise from doc changes mixed with code changes | ~8 files + ~15-20 source/tool files — Risk: missing shifts, commit granularity decay, verification bias | Rec when boundary shifts are known-known (e.g., removing a specific deprecated file whose CQRS role is already documented — no discovery needed) |
| **Cleanup-first-then-sync** — Full dead-code + context-rot pass, then separate sub-phase to remap all manifests from the cleaned state | Cleanest delta between old manifest and new reality; manifests reflect post-cleanup truth; sub-phase has a single clear scope: "make maps match code" | Temporary drift period where manifests are stale relative to actual code; risk of cleanup violating a CQRS constraint that a manifest would have caught; two separate git operations + review cycles | ~6 manifest files (ARCHITECTURE, STRUCTURE, AGENTS.md × 2-3 primary, CONCERNS) — Risk: mid-phase CQRS violations unnoticed, drift window | Rec if CQRS/dependency boundaries are stable and cleanup targets are pure deletions (dead code, deprecated wrappers) — no boundary-discovery needed |

**Rationale:** This project's codebase maps track `last_mapped_commit` (ARCHITECTURE.md, STRUCTURE.md at commit `906b21a0`) and CONCERNS.md already documents known CQRS violations (CommandDelegationHandler mixing observe vs dispatch, hooks doing durable writes) plus stale map drift as a fragility area. Given these known-unknown boundary issues, **verify-then-cleanup** is the recommended approach when boundary shifts are suspected but not quantified, because the pre-audit catches undocumented drifts before cleanup code touches CQRS boundaries — critical in a project where `src/hooks/composition/cqrs-boundary.ts` is an explicit gate. Use **batch cleanup+sync** only when the cleanup targets are pure deletions of files whose boundary role is already fully documented (e.g., removing a deprecated `manager-runtime.ts` adapter). Use **cleanup-first-then-sync** only when the cleanup scope is strictly filesystem-level (dead imports, unused exports, empty directories) with zero CQRS or dependency-boundary impact — which is unlikely given this project's tightly coupled delegation/continuity/lifecycle layers.
